pages.name: oscmd_1
pages.title: Path B — OS Command Injection | Step 1: Find the Injection Point

elements.name: oscmd_context
elements.html: The web portal at 192.168.1.55 has a diagnostic endpoint — it appears to take user input and pass it directly to the server. This could be a way in. You need to confirm whether it's vulnerable before committing to this path.

elements.name: oscmd_discover
elements.title: How do you test the diagnostic endpoint?

choices.text: Send a quick manual test with an unexpected character and watch how the server responds [costs 1 min]
Run an automated scanner against the endpoint [costs 3 min]
Try a different separator and see if anything unexpected leaks into the response [costs 1 min]
Use a dedicated detection tool to auto-probe the endpoint [costs 2 min]

pages.name: oscmd_2
pages.title: Path B — OS Command Injection | Step 2: Establish Access

elements.name: oscmd_confirmed
elements.html: Injection is confirmed — the server is processing your input unsafely and output is leaking back. You have a foothold, but it's fragile. You need to upgrade to something stable so you can move around freely.

elements.name: oscmd_shell
elements.title: How do you turn this into persistent access?

choices.text: Use a callback payload to establish a connection back to your listener [costs 2 min]
Use a scripting-language payload — more portable across different server setups [costs 2 min]
Use a pipe-based approach suited for limited environments [costs 3 min]
Drop a remotely-accessible file onto the server's web root [costs 4 min]

pages.name: oscmd_3
pages.title: Path B — OS Command Injection | Step 3: Escalate Privileges

elements.name: oscmd_shell_ok
elements.html: You're in, but running as a low-privilege service account. You can't reach the transfer system from here. You need to find a path to higher privileges — or credentials that get you where you need to go.

elements.name: oscmd_privesc
elements.title: How do you attempt to escalate your access?

choices.text: Look through application config files — services often store credentials in plaintext [costs 1 min]
Search for system binaries with elevated permissions that might be abusable [costs 3 min]
Check what commands your current account is permitted to run with elevated rights [costs 1 min]
Look for scheduled tasks running as a higher-privileged user that you might influence [costs 2 min]

pages.name: oscmd_branch_suid
pages.title: Path B — OS Command Injection | Dead End

elements.name: oscmd_suid_result
elements.html: Nothing here is exploitable — the system is patched and locked down on that front. You've spent time chasing a dead end. You'll need to pivot to a different angle before the clock runs out.

choices.text: Fall back to reading application config files for stored credentials [costs 2 min]
Fall back to checking scheduled tasks for a writable privileged script [costs 2 min]

pages.name: oscmd_4
pages.title: Path B — OS Command Injection | Step 4: Reach the Transfer System

elements.name: oscmd_db_creds
elements.html: You found stored credentials for a service account with direct access to the transfer approval system. You now have everything you need. How do you use them?

elements.name: oscmd_final
elements.title: How do you use the credentials to reach your objective?

choices.text: Access the backend directly and submit a pre-approved transfer record [costs 1 min]
Grab an active session token and impersonate a logged-in user in the portal [costs 2 min]
Modify account records directly in the backend [costs 3 min]

pages.name: oscmd_fail
pages.title: Path B — OS Command Injection | Complete

elements.name: oscmd_win
elements.html:
// ACCESS GRANTED

Starting from a single vulnerable endpoint, you escalated access, recovered credentials, and reached the transfer system.

Path B finished. Your time and decisions have been logged.
